author: AsyaPronina
title: JS API E2E test after-all hook fails with rm Permission Denied on Windows Electron files
signature: rm cannot remove electron.exe Permission denied|Flaky Test

failed_jobs.name: JS API (22)
failed_jobs.symptom: E2E after-all cleanup hook fails: rm cannot remove electron.exe/DLLs - Permission denied

key_errors:
exec error: Error: Command failed: rm -rf demo-electron-app-project
rm: cannot remove 'demo-electron-app-project/node_modules/electron/dist/d3dcompiler_47.dll': Permission denied
rm: cannot remove 'demo-electron-app-project/node_modules/electron/dist/electron.exe': Permission denied
rm: cannot remove 'demo-electron-app-project/node_modules/electron/dist/libEGL.dll': Permission denied
rm: cannot remove 'demo-electron-app-project/node_modules/electron/dist/libGLESv2.dll': Permission denied
root_cause: The mocha 'after all' cleanup hook runs 'rm -rf demo-electron-app-project' (via git-bash rm) to clean up the test directory after the E2E Electron test. On Windows, the Electron process (electron.exe) and its associated DLLs (d3dcompiler_47.dll, libEGL.dll, libGLESv2.dll) are still held open by the OS or a child process when the cleanup runs, causing a Permission Denied error. The tests themselves pass (2/2); only the cleanup hook fails. This is a Windows file-locking issue caused by the Electron process not fully terminating before cleanup is attempted.
reproduction_steps: Run 'npm run test:e2e' twice consecutively on Windows (or in a CI pipeline where the cleanup from a prior run leaves locked Electron binaries); the second run's after-hook will fail if Electron did not fully exit.
recommended_actions: Change the cleanup command from 'rm -rf' (POSIX) to use 'Remove-Item -Recurse -Force' or 'rimraf' (which handles Windows file locks) in the after-all hook.
Add a delay or retry loop before cleanup to ensure the Electron process has fully exited.
Use 'taskkill /F /IM electron.exe' before 'rm -rf' to forcefully terminate the Electron process on Windows.
prevention_strategies: Always use cross-platform file removal tools (e.g., 'rimraf') in cleanup hooks rather than POSIX 'rm -rf' when tests run on Windows, especially for directories containing executable files that may still be running.
historical_context: First occurrence of this pattern. Windows file-locking during Electron cleanup is a known issue in Node.js test suites using mocha.
